question:
Let $a$ and $b$ be real numbers in the interval $(0,1/2)$, and
let $g$ be a continuous real-valued function such that
$g(g(x))= ag(x)+bx$ for all real $x$.  Prove that
$g(x)=cx$ for some constant $c$.
solution:
Note that $g(x) = g(y)$ implies that $g(g(x)) = g(g(y))$ and hence
$x = y$ from the given equation. That is, $g$ is injective. Since $g$
is also continuous, $g$ is either strictly increasing or strictly
decreasing. Moreover, $g$ cannot tend to a finite limit $L$ as $x \to
+\infty$, or else we'd have $g(g(x)) - ag(x) = bx$, with the left side
bounded and the right side unbounded. Similarly, $g$ cannot tend to
a finite limit as $x \to -\infty$. Together with monotonicity, this
yields that $g$ is also surjective.

Pick $x_0$ arbitrary, and define $x_n$ for all $n \in \ZZ$ recursively
by $x_{n+1} = g(x_n)$ for $n > 0$, and $x_{n-1} = g^{-1}(x_n)$ for $n<0$.
Let $r_1 = (a + \sqrt{a^2+4b})/2$ and $r_2 = (a - \sqrt{a^2+4b})/2$ and
$r_2$ be the roots of $x^2 - ax-b = 0$, so that $r_1 > 0 >
r_2$ and $1 > |r_1| > |r_2|$. Then there exist $c_1, c_2 \in \RR$ such that
$x_n = c_1 r_1^n + c_2 r_2^n$ for all $n \in \ZZ$.

Suppose $g$ is strictly increasing. If $c_2 \neq 0$ for some choice of
$x_0$, then $x_n$ is dominated by $r_2^n$ for $n$ sufficiently
negative. But taking $x_n$ and $x_{n+2}$ for $n$ sufficiently negative of the
right parity, we get $0 < x_n < x_{n+2}$ but $g(x_n) > g(x_{n+2})$,
contradiction. Thus $c_2 = 0$; since $x_0 = c_1$
and $x_1 = c_1 r_1$, we have $g(x) = r_1 x$ for all $x$.
Analogously, if $g$ is strictly decreasing, then $c_2 = 0$ or else
$x_n$ is dominated by $r_1^n$ for $n$ sufficiently positive. But taking
$x_n$ and $x_{n+2}$ for $n$ sufficiently positive of the right parity,
we get $0 < x_{n+2} <x_n$ but $g(x_{n+2}) < g(x_n)$, contradiction.
Thus in that case, $g(x) = r_2 x$ for all $x$.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $consta$ and $constb$ be real numbers in the interval $(0,1/2)$, and
let $mapping$ be a continuous real-valued function such that
$mapping(mapping(variable))= consta\,mapping(variable)+constb\,variable$ for all real $variable$.  Prove that
$mapping(variable)=scalar\,variable$ for some constant $scalar$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Note that $mapping(variable) = mapping(another)$ implies that $mapping(mapping(variable)) = mapping(mapping(another))$ and hence
$variable = another$ from the given equation. That is, $mapping$ is injective. Since $mapping$
is also continuous, $mapping$ is either strictly increasing or strictly
decreasing. Moreover, $mapping$ cannot tend to a finite limit $limitl$ as $variable \to
+\infty$, or else we'd have $mapping(mapping(variable)) - consta\,mapping(variable) = constb\,variable$, with the left side
bounded and the right side unbounded. Similarly, $mapping$ cannot tend to
a finite limit as $variable \to -\infty$. Together with monotonicity, this
yields that $mapping$ is also surjective.

Pick $initial$ arbitrary, and define $sequence$ for all $counter \in \ZZ$ recursively
by $variable_{counter+1} = mapping(variable_{counter})$ for $counter > 0$, and $variable_{counter-1} = mapping^{-1}(variable_{counter})$ for $counter<0$.
Let $rootone = (consta + \sqrt{consta^2+4\,constb})/2$ and $roottwo = (consta - \sqrt{consta^2+4\,constb})/2$ and
$roottwo$ be the roots of $variable^2 - consta\,variable-constb = 0$, so that $rootone > 0 >
roottwo$ and $1 > |rootone| > |roottwo|$. Then there exist $coeffone, coefftwo \in \RR$ such that
$sequence = coeffone\,rootone^{counter} + coefftwo\,roottwo^{counter}$ for all $counter \in \ZZ$.

Suppose $mapping$ is strictly increasing. If $coefftwo \neq 0$ for some choice of
$initial$, then $sequence$ is dominated by $roottwo^{counter}$ for $counter$ sufficiently
negative. But taking $sequence$ and $variable_{counter+2}$ for $counter$ sufficiently negative of the
right parity, we get $0 < sequence < variable_{counter+2}$ but $mapping(sequence) > mapping(variable_{counter+2})$,
contradiction. Thus $coefftwo = 0$; since $initial = coeffone$
and $variable_1 = coeffone\,rootone$, we have $mapping(variable) = rootone\,variable$ for all $variable$.
Analogously, if $mapping$ is strictly decreasing, then $coefftwo = 0$ or else
$sequence$ is dominated by $rootone^{counter}$ for $counter$ sufficiently positive. But taking
$sequence$ and $variable_{counter+2}$ for $counter$ sufficiently positive of the right parity,
we get $0 < variable_{counter+2} < sequence$ but $mapping(variable_{counter+2}) < mapping(sequence)$, contradiction.
Thus in that case, $mapping(variable) = roottwo\,variable$ for all $variable$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $lighthouse$ and $paintbrush$ be real numbers in the interval $(0,1/2)$, and
let $marshmallow$ be a continuous real-valued function such that
$marshmallow(marshmallow(raincloud))= lighthousemarshmallow(raincloud)+paintbrushraincloud$ for all real $raincloud$.  Prove that
$marshmallow(raincloud)= snowcastleraincloud$ for some constant $snowcastle$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Note that $marshmallow(raincloud) = marshmallow(sunflower)$ implies that $marshmallow(marshmallow(raincloud)) = marshmallow(marshmallow(sunflower))$ and hence
$raincloud = sunflower$ from the given equation. That is, $marshmallow$ is injective. Since $marshmallow$
is also continuous, $marshmallow$ is either strictly increasing or strictly
decreasing. Moreover, $marshmallow$ cannot tend to a finite limit $jellybeans$ as $raincloud \to
+\infty$, or else we'd have $marshmallow(marshmallow(raincloud)) - lighthousemarshmallow(raincloud) = paintbrushraincloud$, with the left side
bounded and the right side unbounded. Similarly, $marshmallow$ cannot tend to
a finite limit as $raincloud \to -\infty$. Together with monotonicity, this
yields that $marshmallow$ is also surjective.

Pick $raincloudzero$ arbitrary, and define $raincloudn$ for all $crocodile \in \ZZ$ recursively
by $raincloud_{crocodile+1} = marshmallow(raincloudn)$ for $crocodile > 0$, and $raincloud_{crocodile-1} = marshmallow^{-1}(raincloudn)$ for $crocodile<0$.
Let $peppermint = (lighthouse + \sqrt{lighthouse^2+4paintbrush})/2$ and $butterscotch = (lighthouse - \sqrt{lighthouse^2+4paintbrush})/2$ and
$butterscotch$ be the roots of $raincloud^2 - lighthouseraincloud-paintbrush = 0$, so that $peppermint > 0 >
butterscotch$ and $1 > |peppermint| > |butterscotch|$. Then there exist $marigold, chandelier \in \RR$ such that
$raincloudn = marigold\,peppermint^{crocodile} + chandelier\,butterscotch^{crocodile}$ for all $crocodile \in \ZZ$.

Suppose $marshmallow$ is strictly increasing. If $chandelier \neq 0$ for some choice of
$raincloudzero$, then $raincloudn$ is dominated by $butterscotch^{crocodile}$ for $crocodile$ sufficiently
negative. But taking $raincloudn$ and $raincloud_{crocodile+2}$ for $crocodile$ sufficiently negative of the
right parity, we get $0 < raincloudn < raincloud_{crocodile+2}$ but $marshmallow(raincloudn) > marshmallow(raincloud_{crocodile+2})$,
contradiction. Thus $chandelier = 0$; since $raincloudzero = marigold$
and $raincloud_1 = marigold\peppermint$, we have $marshmallow(raincloud) = \peppermint raincloud$ for all $raincloud$.
Analogously, if $marshmallow$ is strictly decreasing, then $chandelier = 0$ or else
$raincloudn$ is dominated by $peppermint^{crocodile}$ for $crocodile$ sufficiently positive. But taking
$raincloudn$ and $raincloud_{crocodile+2}$ for $crocodile$ sufficiently positive of the right parity,
we get $0 < raincloud_{crocodile+2} < raincloudn$ but $marshmallow(raincloud_{crocodile+2}) < marshmallow(raincloudn)$, contradiction.
Thus in that case, $marshmallow(raincloud) = butterscotch raincloud$ for all $raincloud$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $hugevalue$ and $giantvalue$ be real numbers in the interval $(0,1/2)$, and
let $constantmap$ be a continuous real-valued function such that
$constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue))= hugevalue constantmap(knownvalue)+giantvalue knownvalue$ for all real $knownvalue$.  Prove that
$constantmap(knownvalue)=variableconst knownvalue$ for some constant $variableconst$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Note that $constantmap(knownvalue) = constantmap(fixedvalue)$ implies that $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue)) = constantmap(constantmap(fixedvalue))$ and hence
$knownvalue = fixedvalue$ from the given equation. That is, $constantmap$ is injective. Since $constantmap$
is also continuous, $constantmap$ is either strictly increasing or strictly
decreasing. Moreover, $constantmap$ cannot tend to a finite limit $boundless$ as $knownvalue \to
+\infty$, or else we'd have $constantmap(constantmap(knownvalue)) - hugevalue\,constantmap(knownvalue) = giantvalue\,knownvalue$, with the left side
bounded and the right side unbounded. Similarly, $constantmap$ cannot tend to
a finite limit as $knownvalue \to -\infty$. Together with monotonicity, this
yields that $constantmap$ is also surjective.

Pick $finalpoint$ arbitrary, and define $uniformvalue$ for all $continuumindex \in \ZZ$ recursively
by $uniformvalue_{\,continuumindex+1} = constantmap(uniformvalue_{\,continuumindex})$ for $continuumindex > 0$, and $uniformvalue_{\,continuumindex-1} = constantmap^{-1}(uniformvalue_{\,continuumindex})$ for $continuumindex<0$.
Let $leafvalueone = (hugevalue + \sqrt{hugevalue^{2}+4\,giantvalue})/2$ and $leafvaluetwo = (hugevalue - \sqrt{hugevalue^{2}+4\,giantvalue})/2$ be the roots of $knownvalue^{2} - hugevalue\,knownvalue-giantvalue = 0$, so that $leafvalueone > 0 >
leafvaluetwo$ and $1 > |leafvalueone| > |leafvaluetwo|$. Then there exist $unknownone, unknowntwo \in \RR$ such that
$uniformvalue = unknownone\, leafvalueone^{\continuumindex} + unknowntwo\, leafvaluetwo^{\continuumindex}$ for all $continuumindex \in \ZZ$.

Suppose $constantmap$ is strictly increasing. If $unknowntwo \neq 0$ for some choice of
$finalpoint$, then $uniformvalue$ is dominated by $leafvaluetwo^{\continuumindex}$ for $\continuumindex$ sufficiently
negative. But taking $uniformvalue$ and $uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2}$ for $\continuumindex$ sufficiently negative of the
right parity, we get $0 < uniformvalue < uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2}$ but $constantmap(uniformvalue) > constantmap(uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2})$,
contradiction. Thus $unknowntwo = 0$; since $finalpoint = unknownone$
and $uniformvalue_{1} = unknownone\, leafvalueone$, we have $constantmap(knownvalue) = leafvalueone\, knownvalue$ for all $knownvalue$.
Analogously, if $constantmap$ is strictly decreasing, then $unknowntwo = 0$ or else
$uniformvalue$ is dominated by $leafvalueone^{\continuumindex}$ for $\continuumindex$ sufficiently positive. But taking
$uniformvalue$ and $uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2}$ for $\continuumindex$ sufficiently positive of the right parity,
we get $0 < uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2} < uniformvalue$ but $constantmap(uniformvalue_{\,\continuumindex+2}) < constantmap(uniformvalue)$, contradiction.
Thus in that case, $constantmap(knownvalue) = leafvaluetwo\, knownvalue$ for all $knownvalue$. }

garbled_string.question:
Let $ujkntrap$ and $flovregi$ be real numbers in the interval $(0,1/2)$, and
let $zqrvuwnm$ be a continuous real-valued function such that
$zqrvuwnm(zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa))= ujkntrap zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa)+ flovregi ptlshgfa$ for all real $ptlshgfa$.  Prove that
$zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa)= snirwopq ptlshgfa$ for some constant $snirwopq$.
garbled_string.solution:
Note that $zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa) = zqrvuwnm(nbkduqes)$ implies that $zqrvuwnm(zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa)) = zqrvuwnm(zqrvuwnm(nbkduqes))$ and hence
$ptlshgfa = nbkduqes$ from the given equation. That is, $zqrvuwnm$ is injective. Since $zqrvuwnm$
is also continuous, $zqrvuwnm$ is either strictly increasing or strictly
decreasing. Moreover, $zqrvuwnm$ cannot tend to a finite limit $gmitzsoe$ as $ptlshgfa \to
+\infty$, or else we'd have $zqrvuwnm(zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa)) - ujkntrap zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa) = flovregi ptlshgfa$, with the left side
bounded and the right side unbounded. Similarly, $zqrvuwnm$ cannot tend to
a finite limit as $ptlshgfa \to -\infty$. Together with monotonicity, this
yields that $zqrvuwnm$ is also surjective.

Pick $orvaxmnl$ arbitrary, and define $bqedsplk$ for all $hjmwrvca \in \ZZ$ recursively
by $ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+1} = zqrvuwnm(bqedsplk)$ for $hjmwrvca > 0$, and $ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca-1} = zqrvuwnm^{-1}(bqedsplk)$ for $hjmwrvca<0$.
Let $klydseqv = (ujkntrap + \sqrt{ujkntrap^2+4flovregi})/2$ and $qopnrzxa = (ujkntrap - \sqrt{ujkntrap^2+4flovregi})/2$ and
$qopnrzxa$ be the roots of $ptlshgfa^2 - ujkntrap ptlshgfa-flovregi = 0$, so that $klydseqv > 0 >
qopnrzxa$ and $1 > |klydseqv| > |qopnrzxa|$. Then there exist $dvmxheku, mtrsejga \in \RR$ such that
$bqedsplk = dvmxheku klydseqv^{hjmwrvca} + mtrsejga qopnrzxa^{hjmwrvca}$ for all $hjmwrvca \in \ZZ$.

Suppose $zqrvuwnm$ is strictly increasing. If $mtrsejga \neq 0$ for some choice of
$orvaxmnl$, then $bqedsplk$ is dominated by $qopnrzxa^{hjmwrvca}$ for $hjmwrvca$ sufficiently
negative. But taking $bqedsplk$ and $ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2}$ for $hjmwrvca$ sufficiently negative of the
right parity, we get $0 < bqedsplk < ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2}$ but $zqrvuwnm(bqedsplk) > zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2})$,
contradiction. Thus $mtrsejga = 0$; since $orvaxmnl = dvmxheku$
and $ptlshgfa_1 = dvmxheku klydseqv$, we have $zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa) = klydseqv\, ptlshgfa$ for all $ptlshgfa$.
Analogously, if $zqrvuwnm$ is strictly decreasing, then $mtrsejga = 0$ or else
$bqedsplk$ is dominated by $klydseqv^{hjmwrvca}$ for $hjmwrvca$ sufficiently positive. But taking
$bqedsplk$ and $ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2}$ for $hjmwrvca$ sufficiently positive of the right parity,
we get $0 < ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2} < bqedsplk$ but $zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa_{hjmwrvca+2}) < zqrvuwnm(bqedsplk)$, contradiction.
Thus in that case, $zqrvuwnm(ptlshgfa) = qopnrzxa\, ptlshgfa$ for all $ptlshgfa$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let real numbers a,b satisfy
   1/3 < a < 3/4 ,  1/2 < b < 4/5 ,  a + b \neq 1.

Let f : \mathbb R \to \mathbb R be a continuous function that obeys the functional equation
   f(f(x)) = a \,f(x) + b \,x \qquad(\forall x \in \mathbb R).

Prove that there exists a real constant c - depending only on a and b - such that
   f(x)= c \,x \qquad(\forall x \in \mathbb R).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof we put
        r_1:=\frac{a+\sqrt{a^{2}+4b}}{2},\qquad r_2:=\frac{a-\sqrt{a^{2}+4b}}{2},
so that r_1,r_2 are the (real) roots of  t^{2}-a t-b=0.

STEP 1 - position of the roots.
Because b>0 we have r_1>r_2 and r_1 r_2=-b<0, hence r_1>0>r_2.

(1a)  The inequality |r_2|<1.
We have |r_2|=(\sqrt{a^{2}+4b}-a)/2.  Since |r_2|<1 is equivalent to
        \sqrt{a^{2}+4b} < a+2  \iff  4b < 4a+4  \iff  b < a+1.
The last inequality is true because b<4/5 and a>1/3 imply b<a+1.
Thus |r_2|<1.

(1b)  The value r_1 is different from 1.
Indeed r_1=1 would give r_2=a-1 and hence a+b=1, contradicting the hypothesis.
Consequently either 0<r_1<1 or r_1>1 may occur.

STEP 2 - injectivity, monotonicity, surjectivity of f.

Injectivity.  If f(x)=f(y) then
   b(x-y)=f(f(x))-af(x)-\bigl(f(f(y))-af(y)\bigr)=0\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;x=y,
so f is injective.

Monotonicity.  A continuous injective map is strictly monotone, hence f is either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing.

No finite horizontal asymptote.  Suppose for definiteness that \lim_{x\to+\infty}f(x)=L\in \mathbb R.  Then the left-hand side of
   f(f(x))-af(x)=bx
is bounded while the right-hand side tends to +\infty - impossible.  The same argument at -\infty rules out a finite limit there.

Surjectivity.  Let us assume f is strictly increasing (the decreasing case is identical) and set K:=f(\mathbb R).  K is an interval, and because f has no finite limit at either end we have \sup K=+\infty and \inf K=-\infty; hence K=\mathbb R and f is surjective.  The same conclusion holds when f is decreasing.

STEP 3 - a linear recurrence along every orbit.
Fix x_0 and define x_{n+1}=f(x_n)   (n\in \mathbb Z).  Because f is bijective the sequence can be indexed over all integers.  Substituting x_n in the functional equation yields
        x_{n+2}=a\,x_{n+1}+b\,x_{n}\qquad(n\in\mathbb Z).   (1)
Since r_1\neq r_2 the general solution of (1) is
        x_n=C_1 r_1^{\,n}+C_2 r_2^{\,n}\qquad(n\in\mathbb Z),   (2)
where C_1,C_2 depend on the chosen orbit.

From now on we treat separately the cases `f increasing' and `f decreasing'.

STEP 4 - f is strictly increasing.
Assume C_2\neq0 for some orbit.  As n\to-\infty the term C_2 r_2^{\,n} dominates in (2) because |r_2|<1 and r_2<0; consequently the sign of x_{n+2}-x_n alternates infinitely often.  Picking n very negative with x_n<x_{n+2} but x_{n+1}>x_{n+3} contradicts the monotonicity of f.  Hence every orbit has C_2=0, so x_{n+1}=r_1 x_n for all n and therefore
        f(x)=r_1x  (x\in\mathbb R).

STEP 5 - f is strictly decreasing.
Put
        c:=r_2(<0), \qquad g(x):=f(x)-c x.
Because c^{2}=ac+b, a short calculation gives
        g(f(x))=(a-c)g(x)=r_1 g(x).      (3)
Two sub-cases arise according to the value of r_1.

5.1  Sub-case r_1>1.
Suppose C_1\neq0 for some orbit (x_n).  Because r_1>1, the term C_1 r_1^{\,n} dominates (2) for large positive n.  Choose N so large that
        |C_2|\,|r_2|^{N}<\frac12 |C_1| r_1^{N}.     (4)
Then for all n\ge N we have
        x_n=C_1 r_1^{n}(1+\varepsilon_n), \quad |\varepsilon_n|<\tfrac12.   (5)
First difference.  From (2),
        x_{n+2}-x_n=C_1 r_1^{n}(r_1^{2}-1)+O(r_2^{n}),
and by (4) the error term is smaller than half the main term, so the sign of x_{n+2}-x_n is \operatorname{sgn}(C_1).  Because r_1^{2}-1>0, we have
        \operatorname{sgn}(x_{n+2}-x_n)=\operatorname{sgn}(C_1).     (6)
Since f is decreasing, the next iterates satisfy the opposite inequality:
        \operatorname{sgn}(x_{n+3}-x_{n+1})=-\operatorname{sgn}(x_{n+2}-x_n)=-\operatorname{sgn}(C_1).  (7)
Second difference.  Again from (2),
        x_{n+3}-x_{n+1}=C_1 r_1^{n+1}(r_1^{2}-1)+O(r_2^{n+1}),
whose sign, by the same domination estimate, is \operatorname{sgn}(C_1).     (8)
But (7) and (8) together give opposite signs for x_{n+3}-x_{n+1}, a contradiction.  Therefore C_1=0 along every orbit; hence x_{n+1}=r_2 x_n and f(x)=r_2 x.

5.2  Sub-case 0<r_1<1.
The argument is analogous but carried out for large negative indices, as in Step 4.  Assume C_1\neq0 and pick N\ll0 so negative that
        |C_2|\,|r_2|^{N+3}<\tfrac12|C_1| r_1^{N+3}.
Now (5)-(8) remain valid with n\ge N replaced by n\le N and r_1^{2}-1<0, producing again a contradiction.  Hence C_1=0 on every orbit and f(x)=r_2 x.

STEP 6 - conclusion.

If f is increasing we obtained f(x)=r_1x; if f is decreasing we obtained f(x)=r_2x.  In every case there exists a real constant
        c\in\{r_1,r_2\}
depending solely on a and b such that  f(x)=c x  for all real x, as required.

core_steps: Continuity + injectivity ⇒ g is strictly monotone (hence surjective onto ℝ).
Iterates x_{n+2}=a x_{n+1}+b x_n follow from defining x_{n+1}=g(x_n) and the functional equation.
Solve linear recurrence: x_n = c_1 r_1^n + c_2 r_2^n where r_1,r_2 are roots of t^2−a t−b=0.
Monotonicity forces one coefficient 0, giving g(x)=c x with c=r_1 (increasing) or c=r_2 (decreasing).

slot1.description: Numerical upper bound 1/2 for parameters a and b; any bounds that still guarantee b≠0, real distinct roots r1>0, r2<0, and |r1|>|r2|<1 would work.

slot2.description: Choice of the initial seed x_0 used to generate the bi-infinite sequence (x_n); any real x_0 is acceptable.
